behaviors.description: Out-of-stock products excluded from recommendation results
behaviors.justification: A unit test of the filter logic could catch some failures, but the real bug is typically in the query layer (missing WHERE availability=true). The MOCK-MISS risk is high: if we mock the product data to always return pre-filtered results, we hide query bugs. Integration testing with a real/mock product database verifies end-to-end filtering works.
behaviors.test_description: Create a product catalog with 5 in-stock and 3 out-of-stock items. Call recommendations for a user with no special preferences. Assert that all returned products have availability=true and that no products from the known out-of-stock set appear in results by ID.

behaviors.description: User preference filter correctly excludes blocked categories
behaviors.justification: Similar to out-of-stock filtering—the bug is often in query logic. MOCK-HIDE is easy: mock the result set with pre-filtered data and miss the actual filter implementation. Integration testing with a real user profile and product database verifies the filter is applied correctly.
behaviors.test_description: Create a user with blocked categories ['Electronics', 'Toys']. Create a 20-product catalog across 5 categories. Call recommendations. Assert no products from blocked categories appear by checking category fields of all returned products against the blocklist.

behaviors.description: Ranking algorithm weights recent browsing patterns higher than products with no browsing signal
behaviors.justification: While the ranking function could be unit-tested in isolation, the failure often manifests in how browsing history is fetched, loaded, and merged with the ranking logic. MOCK-MISS: if we mock the ranking result to always prefer browsing categories, we hide failures in data fetching. Integration testing verifies the full path: load browsing history → merge with products → apply ranking weights.
behaviors.test_description: Create a user with 3 recent browsing actions in category 'Books'. Create a 25-product catalog with 7 books and 18 non-books. Call recommendations. Assert that at least 5 of the top 10 results are books. Verify that products matching recent browsing categories have higher ranking scores than unrelated products.

behaviors.description: Pagination returns correct page with stable ordering; requesting page 2 twice returns identical results if underlying data hasn't changed
behaviors.justification: Pagination bugs only manifest at realistic scale (100+ items). A unit test on 3 items wouldn't reveal issues with page boundary calculations, sort stability, or offset logic. MOCK-HIDE: mocking the entire result set hides actual pagination implementation. Integration testing with a large dataset verifies stable sorting across multiple calls.
behaviors.test_description: Create a 250-product catalog. Call recommendations with page_size=10, requesting page 1 and page 2 for the same user. Assert they contain different products. Call page 2 twice more without intervening changes. Assert both calls return the exact same product list in the same order, verified by product ID sequences.

behaviors.description: Serendipity requirement: at least one product from a category the user hasn't browsed before
behaviors.justification: Real-world failure: the algorithm fails to diversify recommendations. Testing this requires a real user with established browsing history and a real product catalog. The implementation might use LLM embeddings (Agentic) or algorithmic heuristics (Integration), adding some uncertainty. REAL-INFRA: low. MOCK-HIDE: yes, easily.
behaviors.test_description: Create a user with browsing history in 3 categories (e.g., 'Books', 'Tech', 'Sports'). Create a 30-product catalog spanning 8 categories. Call recommendations. Assert that at least 1 of the top 10 results is from an unbrowsed category. Repeat 3+ times and verify serendipity is always provided (even in presence of randomness/LLM calls).

behaviors.description: New user with no purchase or browsing history receives recommendations based on overall popularity rather than empty result set
behaviors.justification: While the fallback logic is straightforward, integration testing verifies the algorithm correctly detects a new user (empty history) and activates the popularity fallback. MOCK-MISS: easy to mock a new user object and miss edge cases in state detection.
behaviors.test_description: Create a new user with empty browsing and purchase history. Call recommendations. Assert results are non-empty (not null/empty list). Compare returned products to popularity-ranked products from the catalog and verify they align (top results should match the highest-popularity items). Verify no errors occur during fallback.

behaviors.description: Click tracking records correct product ID, position in list, and timestamp for each recommendation interaction
behaviors.justification: The main failure risk is incorrect persistence (product ID stored wrong, position miscalculated, timestamp corrupted, or database write fails). MOCK-MISS: easily hide this by mocking the database. REAL-INFRA: depends on whether the database is in-process (Integration) or external (System). Likely Integration with in-memory or local DB.
behaviors.test_description: Simulate a user click on the 4th product in a recommendation list. Query the click-tracking database. Assert exactly one record exists with product_id matching the 4th product, position=4, and timestamp within 5 seconds of now(). Verify all three fields are correctly persisted and can be retrieved.

behaviors.description: Response time for generating recommendations stays under 200ms for a catalog of 100,000 products
behaviors.justification: Performance bugs only manifest at realistic scale. MOCK-MISS: absolutely—mocking the algorithm or data loading hides slow implementations. REAL-INFRA: depends on whether catalog is loaded from memory, disk, or database. Either way, you need a realistic 100k-product dataset. Integration with in-memory 100k items is minimum; System if file I/O is involved.
behaviors.test_description: Load exactly 100,000 products into the recommendation system. Call recommendations for a typical user with browsing history and preferences. Measure end-to-end response time over 10+ iterations. Assert 95th percentile response time < 200ms (or all runs < 200ms depending on SLA strictness). Use consistent cold/warm cache behavior.

behaviors.description: When a recommended product is purchased, the recommendation algorithm deprioritizes it in subsequent requests
behaviors.justification: Like browsing-pattern ranking, the failure is often in how purchase history is fetched and integrated with the ranking logic. MOCK-MISS: mock purchase data and hide missing deprioritization logic. REAL-INFRA: no. Integration testing verifies the full pipeline: load purchase history → apply deprioritization weights.
behaviors.test_description: Create a user with 2 purchased products [ProductX, ProductY]. Create a 20-product catalog with ProductX, ProductY, and 18 similar products. Call recommendations. Assert that ProductX and ProductY, if present, are ranked below position 10 (significantly deprioritized compared to unpurchased similar products). Verify deprioritization across multiple calls with the same user state.

behaviors.description: Recommendation results are deterministic for the same user state; requesting recommendations twice with no intervening user activity returns the same ranked list
behaviors.justification: Determinism failures manifest only when you call the algorithm twice and compare results. MOCK-MISS: easy to hide non-determinism with a mock. REAL-INFRA: no, purely in-process. If the algorithm uses unseeded randomness or real LLM calls, it's Agentic (and behavior may not be satisfiable). Assuming deterministic implementation, Integration is the minimum.
behaviors.test_description: Call recommendations for a user with established browsing and purchase history. Store the returned product list (IDs in order). Call recommendations again with no intervening user activity. Assert the returned list is identical: same product IDs in the same order. Repeat 3+ times across different user states to verify determinism is consistent and reliable.
